title: After The Line
description: Structured debrief, not rumination. Process the race, then put it away.

blocks.content: The finish line you cross with your body is the easy one. It's the line you cross with your brain — 24 hours later, sitting with the result — that most athletes never prepare for.

Good race? The high lasts an hour, maybe two. Then a flatness settles in — bizarrely close to sadness. The thing that gave your days structure is in the rearview mirror. Bad race? The spiral starts immediately. Before you've left the finish area, the chimp has hijacked the analysis: 'I wasn't fit enough. I'm not cut out for this.' Either way, what happens in the 24 hours after matters more than most athletes realize.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a structured Post-Race Debrief framework.

blocks.question: Nutritional anxiety is:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Nutritional anxiety is formally identified as a distinct psychological construct. You can eat perfectly and still suffer performance consequences from the anxiety alone.

options.text: Just being nervous about eating before a race
A distinct psychological construct that affects performance independently of actual fuel intake
A sign you haven't trained enough

blocks.content: Structured debrief, not rumination. Reflection is purposeful analysis with a beginning, middle, and end. Rumination is repetitive, self-critical thought that circles the same drain. The difference isn't the content — it's the structure.

items.title: Post-Event Emotional Crash
items.content: After any major event — good or bad — athletes are psychologically vulnerable. You're physically depleted (low glycogen = low prefrontal function), emotionally raw (hours of effort strip psychological defenses), and you have nothing to do (training paused, event over). The brain occupies itself with rumination.

items.title: Rumination vs. Reflection
items.content: Nolen-Hoeksema et al. (2008): rumination doesn't just fail to help — it actively worsens mood, increases depression symptoms, and impairs future problem-solving. People who ruminate after negative events perform WORSE on subsequent challenges. The rumination doesn't just feel bad. It makes you worse.

items.title: Positive Reflection → Integration → Move On
items.content: Athletes who completed formal debriefing protocols performed better in subsequent competitions (Thelwell, Greenlees, & Weston, 2006). The debrief wasn't just emotional first aid — it was a learning tool that converted experience into improvement. Without the structured bridge, athletes fall into the gap.

items.label: What went well?
items.detail: Start here. Always. Even if the race was a disaster — especially then. Something went well. You showed up. You deployed the Grit Stack at mile 60. You ate on schedule for three hours. This is deliberate evidence collection for your Evidence File. If you don't catalog what went well, the chimp catalogs what went wrong.

items.label: What was hard?
items.detail: Not 'what went wrong.' What was HARD. The language matters. 'What went wrong' invites self-blame. 'What was hard' invites honest assessment without judgment. Where did the chimp get loud? Where did your plan break? Neutral observation — the non-judging facet of mindfulness.

items.label: What would I do differently?
items.detail: Now — and only now — you problem-solve. Not 'I should have been tougher.' What, specifically? 'I would start the Grit Stack earlier.' 'I would add an If-Then for the headwind section.' 'I would add a 5-minute buffer to my pre-race protocol.' Actionable. Specific. Forward-looking.

items.label: What am I proud of?
items.detail: End here. Always. Not the same as Question 1 — that was analytical. This is emotional. What do you respect about yourself for having done this? This deposits directly into your self-concept. 'I am the kind of athlete who shows up with a plan' is a very different identity entry than 'I always go out too hard.'

blocks.content: The rules: Complete within 24 hours. Write it — not in your head. Thinking about it is rumination; writing is reflection. Then close it. The debrief has a beginning, a middle, and an end.
Commitment: I will complete a written Post-Race Debrief within 24 hours of my next race.
Write it. Close it. You are not your last result.
Module 5 complete. You have the race-day protocol.
Rate yourself again: How structured is your mental race-day preparation? Compare to your score at the start of Module 5.
